meta.title: Introduction to Sets
meta.subject: math
meta.description: Learn what a set is, how to write sets using different methods, types of sets, and relationships between sets.

items.back: A set is a well-defined collection of objects. 'Well-defined' means we can clearly say whether any object belongs or not. Example: {2, 4, 6, 8} is a set of even numbers less than 10.
items.hint: Can you always say YES or NO if something belongs?

items.back: ∈ means 'belongs to' or 'is a member of'. If P = {2, 3, 5, 7}, then 2 ∈ P means '2 belongs to set P'.
items.hint: ∈ looks like 'E' — think Element

items.back: ∉ means 'does not belong to'. If P = {2, 3, 5, 7}, then 4 ∉ P means '4 does not belong to set P'.
items.hint: ∉ is ∈ crossed out — NOT an element

items.back: In the listing method, all members are written inside curly braces { }, separated by commas. Example: A = {1, 4, 9} — the square numbers less than 10. Each member is written only once.
items.hint: List every member inside { }, no repeats

items.back: In the description method, the common property of members is written inside braces. Example: A = {square numbers less than 10}, B = {factors of 18}.
items.hint: Describe WHAT the members have in common

items.back: A variable and its property are written inside braces. Example: A = {x : x is a square number less than 10}. Read as: 'A is the set of all x such that x is a square number less than 10'.
items.hint: Format is {x : x has this property}

items.front: What is the Cardinal Number of a set?
items.back: The cardinal number is the total count of elements in a set, written as n(A). If A = {1, 4, 9}, then n(A) = 3. An empty set has n(A) = 0.
items.hint: Count the members → that is n(A)

items.back: An empty or null set has no elements, written as { } or ø. Example: A = {oceans in Nepal} = { } because Nepal has no oceans. n(A) = 0.
items.hint: No members at all — written as { } or ø

items.back: A singleton or unit set has exactly one element. Example: A = {the highest peak of the world} = {Sagarmatha}, so n(A) = 1.
items.hint: Exactly ONE member inside

items.front: Finite vs Infinite Set — what is the difference?
items.back: A finite set has a countable number of elements that comes to an end. Example: W = {0,1,2,...,9}, n(W) = 10. An infinite set never ends. Example: N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ...} — shown by '...'.
items.hint: Finite = counting stops. Infinite = never ends (look for ...)

items.front: What are Equal Sets and Equivalent Sets?
items.back: Equal sets have exactly the same elements: A = {e,f,g,h} and B = {g,f,h,e} → A = B. Equivalent sets have the same number of elements but different members: P = {1,3,5,7} and Q = {2,4,6,8} → P ~ Q (both have n = 4).
items.hint: Equal = same elements. Equivalent = same COUNT.

items.front: What are Overlapping Sets and Disjoint Sets?
items.back: Overlapping sets share at least one common element. Example: A = {a,e,i,o,u} and B = {a,b,c,d} share 'a'. Disjoint sets share NO common elements. Example: M = {5,10,15,20} and N = {2,4,6,8} are disjoint.
items.hint: Overlapping = share something. Disjoint = share nothing.

items.question: Which of the following is a well-defined collection (a set)?
options: A collection of tall students in Class 6
A collection of nice Nepali songs
items.explanation: A collection of fruits is well-defined — we can clearly say whether any object is a fruit. 'Tasty', 'tall', and 'nice' are subjective and make the collection NOT well-defined.
items.hint: Can we clearly say YES or NO for every object?

items.question: If N = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, is the statement '4 ∈ O' True or False?
items.explanation: 4 is not in set O = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9}, so 4 ∉ O. The statement '4 ∈ O' is False.
items.hint: Check if 4 appears in set O

items.question: If W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} and E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, which symbol fills the blank: 6 ___ E?
items.explanation: 6 is in set E = {2, 4, 6, 8}, so 6 ∈ E (6 belongs to set E).
items.hint: Is 6 one of the members of set E?

items.question: If A = {prime numbers between 10 and 20}, which is the correct listing of set A?
items.explanation: Prime numbers between 10 and 20: 11, 13, 17, 19. (15 = 3×5 is composite; 12, 14, 16, 18, 20 are also composite.)
items.hint: A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself

items.question: If D = {y : y is a factor of 12}, which is the correct listing of D?
items.explanation: Factors of 12 are all numbers that divide 12 exactly: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12.
items.hint: Which numbers divide 12 with no remainder?

items.question: Let W = {whole numbers less than 6}. Which is the correct listing?
items.explanation: Whole numbers start from 0. Whole numbers less than 6 are: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.
items.hint: Whole numbers start from 0, not 1

items.question: What is the set-builder form of W = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}?
items.answer: W = {x : x is a whole number, x < 5}
options: W = {x : x is a whole number, x < 5}
W = {x : x is a natural number, x < 5}
W = {x : x is a whole number, x ≤ 5}
W = {x : x is an even number, x < 5}
items.explanation: W contains whole numbers (starting from 0) less than 5, so W = {x : x is a whole number, x < 5}.
items.hint: Whole numbers include 0. What is the highest value in the set?

items.question: What type of set is A = {2, 4, 6, 8, 10, ...}?
items.explanation: The '...' shows the set continues without end. A set with endless elements is an infinite set.
items.hint: Look for '...' — does the counting ever stop?

items.question: If N = {11, 12, 13, 14}, what is n(N)?
items.explanation: n(N) is the cardinal number — the count of elements. N has 4 elements: 11, 12, 13, 14. So n(N) = 4.
items.hint: Count how many members are in the set

items.question: A = {e, f, g, h} and B = {g, f, h, e}. What is the relationship between sets A and B?
items.explanation: A and B contain exactly the same elements (e, f, g, h) just in different order. Two sets with the same elements are equal sets. A = B.
items.hint: Are the members exactly the same — just in different order?

items.question: P = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9} and Q = {2, 4, 6, 8}. What is the relationship between P and Q?
items.explanation: P contains odd numbers and Q contains even numbers. They share no common element, so they are disjoint sets.
items.hint: Do P and Q share any common element?

items.question: What type of set is D = {square numbers between 10 and 20}?
items.explanation: Square numbers between 10 and 20: only 16 (4² = 16). D = {16} has exactly one element, so it is a singleton (unit) set.
items.hint: List the square numbers between 10 and 20 — how many are there?

items.question: What type of set is A = {prime number between 5 and 7}?
items.explanation: Between 5 and 7 strictly, only 6 exists. 6 = 2×3 is not prime. So A = { }, an empty (null) set.
items.hint: List integers strictly between 5 and 7 — is any of them prime?

items.question: If D = {x : x is a square number, x < 75}, what is n(D)?
items.explanation: Square numbers less than 75: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64. That is 8 elements, so n(D) = 8.
items.hint: List 1², 2², 3²... stop when the result reaches 75

items.question: P = {x : x is a prime number, x < 10} and Q = {7, 5, 3, 2}. What is the relationship?
items.explanation: P = {2, 3, 5, 7} and Q = {7, 5, 3, 2}. Both sets contain exactly the same elements in different order. So P = Q (equal sets).
items.hint: List P in order and compare with Q

items.question: A = {factors of 12} and B = {factors of 18}. Are A and B overlapping or disjoint?
items.explanation: A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12} and B = {1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18}. Common elements: {1, 2, 3, 6}. Since they share elements, they are overlapping sets.
items.hint: Find factors of both 12 and 18 — do any appear in both lists?

items.question: P = {first five multiples of 4} and Q = {first five multiples of 7}. Are they overlapping or disjoint?
items.explanation: P = {4, 8, 12, 16, 20} and Q = {7, 14, 21, 28, 35}. No common elements, so they are disjoint sets.
items.hint: List P and Q — does any number appear in both?

items.question: A = {x : x is a natural number less than 1}. What type of set is A?
items.explanation: Natural numbers are 1, 2, 3, ... — no natural number is less than 1. So A = { }, an empty set with n(A) = 0.
items.hint: The smallest natural number is 1 — can any be less than 1?

items.question: If B = {factors of 18}, what is n(B)?
items.explanation: Factors of 18: 1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18. That is 6 factors, so n(B) = 6.
items.hint: Which numbers divide 18 with no remainder? Count them.

items.question: A = {Whole numbers less than 5} and B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}. What is their relationship?
items.explanation: A = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} (whole numbers include 0) and B = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}. Both have n = 5 but different elements. They are equivalent sets (A ~ B), not equal.
items.hint: Remember: whole numbers start from 0. Are the elements exactly the same?
